food_subgroup: Chinese herbal jelly dessert with honey topping

main_ingredients: smilax root (tu fu ling / 土茯苓)

serving.description: 1 bowl (~260g: approximately 250g jelly plus 10g honey topping)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Guilinggao as served at Singapore dessert shops such as Hung Fook Tong, comprising approximately 250g of set herbal jelly topped with one teaspoon (~10g) of honey, totalling approximately 260g.

nutrient_source.title: Guilinggao — HPB Singapore (Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Guilinggao (Chinese herbal jelly with honey). HPB Foodo is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. Values represent the composite dish including honey topping as served.

gi_source.title: Honey GI reference and herbal jelly starch/agar base analogy — University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Guilinggao in the University of Sydney GI Database. The jelly base (agar/starch polymer, herbal extracts, predominantly water) has an estimated GI of approximately 0–10 as it contains negligible digestible carbohydrate. The primary glycaemic contributor is the honey topping (~10g per bowl). Honey GI is approximately 55–58 per University of Sydney GI Database (Food ID 1364, pure honey). The composite GI for the 260g bowl is estimated at approximately 55 (dominated by honey contribution). GL per serving = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (55 × 16.1) / 100 ≈ 8.8. This is a low GL. Estimation basis clearly documented.

hypertension.key_factor: Extremely low sodium (~20mg per 260g bowl); licorice glycyrrhizin content at one bowl per day is clinically insignificant
hypertension.guidance: Guilinggao is suitable for hypertensive patients at one bowl per day; sodium content is negligible (~20mg per serving), however patients consuming multiple bowls daily should be aware that cumulative licorice glycyrrhizin intake may theoretically contribute to pseudohyperaldosteronism (sodium retention, potassium loss, and elevated blood pressure) at doses exceeding 100mg glycyrrhizin per day — one bowl contains less than 10mg glycyrrhizin and is clinically safe.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (~55) and low GL (~8.8 per bowl); very low calorie (~70 kcal per bowl); honey topping is the primary glycaemic driver
type2_diabetes.guidance: Guilinggao is one of the most suitable dessert options for patients with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low calorie content (~70 kcal per 260g bowl), low GI (~55), and low GL (~8.8); requesting minimal or no honey topping reduces GL to approximately 4, making it an excellent low-glycaemic dessert substitute.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero fat, zero saturated fat, zero cholesterol, very low sodium; negligible cardiovascular risk from macronutrient profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Guilinggao presents no cardiovascular concern from its macronutrient profile — it contains zero fat, zero saturated fat, zero cholesterol, and only ~20mg sodium per bowl; it is a heart-friendly dessert option and can be freely consumed by cardiovascular disease patients within a balanced diet.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Zero fat, low calorie; herbal ingredients (smilax root, dandelion, chrysanthemum) have traditional anti-inflammatory use but sub-clinical evidence at commercial doses
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Guilinggao is nutritionally benign for rheumatoid arthritis patients — zero fat, very low calorie, and low sugar; herbal components (smilax root, dandelion luteolin, chrysanthemum apigenin) have traditional anti-inflammatory use in TCM but clinical evidence at commercial serving doses is insufficient to make therapeutic claims.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Guilinggao (龜苓膏, also romanized as Guī Líng Gāo or gwai ling gou in Cantonese) is a traditional Chinese medicinal dessert jelly originating from Wuzhou and Guangdong province, China, and widely consumed across Singapore at dessert chains such as Hung Fook Tong and Eu Yan Sang. Historically prepared from the powdered plastron of the golden coin turtle (Cuora trifasciata, a CITES-protected critically endangered species), all modern commercial versions use smilax root (土茯苓, tu fu ling) as the primary herbal base, combined with chrysanthemum, dandelion root, licorice root, and cornstarch or grass jelly powder. The jelly is set firm, dark brown to black in colour, translucent, with a distinctly bitter and medicinal flavour. It is almost universally served with honey, maple syrup, or condensed milk to offset the bitterness. Per 260g bowl with honey topping: approximately 70 kcal, 0g fat, 0g saturated fat, 0mg cholesterol, 16g total carbohydrate (predominantly from honey), GI approximately 55, GL approximately 8.8, and only ~20mg sodium. It is one of the lowest-calorie desserts available in Singapore. Nutritionally, it is essentially water, herbal extracts, and a small amount of starch, with the honey topping providing the majority of calories and sugar. Marketing claims of 'detox', 'cooling', and skin-clearing effects have no clinical evidence basis at commercial serving doses. Licorice glycyrrhizin content at one bowl per day is clinically safe; patients consuming multiple bowls daily should be aware of potential pseudohyperaldosteronism risk. Suitable for vegetarians and halal diets; not vegan due to honey topping. Source: HPB Singapore Foodo. Confidence: high.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo, the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. HPB Foodo is an authoritative government nutrition database. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database using honey as the primary glycaemic contributor, with the estimation basis fully documented. All mandatory multilingual fields are populated. Ontology uses a defensible closest-parent FoodEx2 code with honest gap notes.
notes: Fat_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo — this is a water-based herbal jelly with no added fat; fat_g is naturally zero by product composition. Cholesterol_mg is naturally zero as this is a plant-based herbal jelly with no animal-derived fat components (modern versions contain no turtle material). Vitamin_a_mcg_rae is confirmed zero per source. Vitamin_b12_mcg is naturally zero as this is a plant-based food. Vitamin_d_mcg is naturally zero as this is a plant-based food with no fortification. Vitamin_e_mg is confirmed zero per source. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat values are not reported by the source and are set to -1. Water_g is not reported by the source and is set to -1. The FoodEx2 code A0GBQ is used as the closest defensible parent category; no exact FoodEx2 code exists for Guilinggao. GI is an analogous estimate based on honey GI (~55) from the University of Sydney GI Database, as no direct GI measurement for Guilinggao exists in any approved GI database. Licorice glycyrrhizin caution applies only at multiple-bowl-per-day consumption patterns. Modern commercial Guilinggao contains no turtle material (CITES-protected Cuora trifasciata). Vegan flag is false due to honey topping; vegetarian flag is true.
